content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 .•• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Mr. Logan Wheatley 
Administrative Specialist 
No. Orange County Community 
College District 
1000 North Lemon Street 
Fullerton, California 
92634 
Dear Mr. Wheatley: 
July 29, 1976 
qfl 
I 
Thank you for your inquiry of July 26, 1976. 
You asked three 
questions related to Conflict of Interest Code interpretation. 
"1. 
Does the adoption (and approval) of a Conflict 
of Interest Code in which a certain designated 
employee is required to report (disclose) an 
investment establish for such employee that 
'the business entity in which the investment 
is held may foreseeably be affected materially 
by any decision made or participated in by the 
designated employee by virtue of his position?'" 
The answer is yes. 
The agency determines that it is foreseeable 
that all categories of interests appearing as disclosure categories 
for the designated employee may be materially affected by the deci-
sion making capacity of the designated employee. 
This determination 
should be made by the agency after a hearing in which the potential 
decision making capacity of the employee and the kinds of investment 
which may be affected by that capacity are carefully examined in 
light of the foreseeability standard. 
By narrowly fashioning dis-
closure categories at this point, future problems of over-disclosure 
will be prevented. 
"2. 
If the answer to the above question is 'yes', 
must the designated employee disqualify him-
self from making or participating in the mak-
ing of any decision in which the business 
entity is involved; or may the designated 
employee base an action to disqua 
or not 
to disqualify himself on his conclusion as 


Mr. Logan Wheatley 
'July 29, 1976 
to whether or not there will be a material 
financial effect (as such term is defined 
in Section 18702, Title 2, Government Code) 
on the entity as a result of his decision?'" 
Page Two 
Your latter characterization is the correct one. 
A 
reported interest which will be but marginally affected, or 
materially affected in a way not distinguishable from its 
effect on the public generally, should not lead to a dis-
qualification situation for a designated employee. 
But 
the fact that the interest is reported will lead the des-
ignated employee to a close examination of whether or not 
it is foreseeable that the individual decision which the 
employee is about to make will. have a material effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, 
on the reported interest. 
Thus, there will be decisional 
situations in which designated employees may affect their 
reportable interests, and yet not be required to disqualify 
themselves from participating in those kinds of decisions. 
The answer to the first two questions obviates the 
necessity for answering the third. 
You ask, however, in 
summation to ,the whole series of questions, "To what extent 
does required disclosure mandate disqualification?" 
The 
simple answer is that "disclosure" and "disqualification" 
are two quite separate concepts in theory and operation. 
Disclosure within a Conflict of Interest Code is designed 
to inform the public and place on notice the public offi-
cial of the kinds of interests held by the official which 
might be affected by the official in his or her decision-
making capacity. 
Disqualification is the duty of every 
public official when one's private interests will clash 
with the performance of one's public duty. 
By the terms 
of Government Code §87103, there are but four kinds of 
private interests which, if affected in a material fashion, 
should lead to disqualification •. Those private interests 
include business entities, real property, and sources of 
income. 
When one of these types of interests, which may 
or may not have been required to be disclosed by the 
public official in a Conflict of Interest Code, will be 
affected by the decision of the public official, disqual-
ification must occur, or the official is in violation of 
Government Code S87100 and the disqualification provisions 
of the Conflict of Interest Code which covers his or her 
position. 


Mr. Logan Wheatley 
July 29, 1976 
Page Three 
Various witnesses have appeared before the Commission to 
intimate that when an interest is disclosed by a designated 
official, the official will automatically disqualify himself 
or herself from participating in any manner which may even 
remotely affect the disclosed interest. 
While disclosure 
may have some such result, it need nbt nor should not. 
Resort 
by the designated officials to the opinions of county counsel, 
code reviewing bodies, agency counsel, and the Commission, 
when their duties are in question should, over the early 
period of adjustment to the law, prevent over-disqualification. 
I hope this response has satisfactorily answered your 
question. 
If you have other questions, please contact this 
office at 916/322-6444 for further guidance. 
DLS:gb 
Sincerely, 
Delbert L_ Spurlock, Jr. 
Chief, Conflicts of Interest 
Division

sections.conclusion: The answer is yes to question one. Your latter characterization is the correct one for question two—a reported interest which will be but marginally affected, or materially affected in a way not distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, should not lead to a disqualification situation. The simple answer to question three is that 'disclosure' and 'disqualification' are two quite separate concepts in theory and operation. Disclosure is designed to inform the public and place on notice the public official of the kinds of interests held which might be affected. Disqualification is the duty of every public official when one's private interests will clash with the performance of one's public duty.
sections.question_synthetic: The inquiry addresses whether adoption of a Conflict of Interest Code requiring disclosure of an investment establishes foreseeability of material effect, whether such disclosure mandates automatic disqualification, and the relationship between disclosure and disqualification requirements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Disclosure of an interest establishes foreseeability of material effect by the designated employee's decisions, but disclosure does not automatically mandate disqualification. Disqualification is required only when a private interest (business entity, real property, or income source) will actually be materially affected by the official's decision in a manner distinguishable from effects on the public generally, as defined under Government Code §87100.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR quality document with clear Q&A structure. Letter dated July 29, 1976 from FPPC Chief responding to July 26 inquiry. Three substantive questions answered with detailed legal analysis. Minor OCR artifacts present in document margins but do not affect extraction of main content.

CONCLUSION: The answer is yes to question one. Your latter characterization is the correct one for question two—a reported interest which will be but marginally affected, or materially affected in a way not distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, should not lead to a disqualification situation. The simple answer to question three is that 'disclosure' and 'disqualification' are two quite separate concepts in theory and operation. Disclosure is designed to inform the public and place on notice the public official of the kinds of interests held which might be affected. Disqualification is the duty of every public official when one's private interests will clash with the performance of one's public duty.

embedding.summary: FPPC Chief Delbert Spurlock responds to questions from North Orange County Community College District regarding Conflict of Interest Code interpretation, clarifying that disclosure and disqualification are separate concepts and that disclosure of reportable interests does not automatically require recusal from all decisions affecting those interests.
